content.body: Reproduction is the production of new individuals to continue the species. It can be sexual (involving two parents and gametes) or asexual (one parent, no gametes).

Asexual reproduction:
• Binary fission (bacteria): one cell splits into two
• Budding (yeast, Hydra): bud grows from parent
• Fragmentation: body breaks, each part regrows
• Vegetative propagation (plants): runners, bulbs, cuttings
• Spore formation (fungi, ferns)

Advantages: fast, no mate needed, all offspring have good genes
Disadvantages: no genetic variation, disease can wipe out all

content.title: Vegetative Propagation
content.body:
Runners: strawberry plants send out horizontal stems
Bulbs: onion stores food, produces new bulbs
Tubers: potato eyes produce new plants
Cuttings: stem cutting develops roots (artificial)
Grafting: join different plant varieties

Sexual reproduction:
Requires male + female gametes (sex cells)
Fertilisation: gametes fuse → zygote → new organism
Produces genetic variation (important for evolution)
Meiosis produces gametes with half the chromosomes

questions.question: Binary fission is reproduction in
questions.explanation: Bacteria reproduce by binary fission — one cell splits into two identical daughter cells.

questions.question: Asexual reproduction requires how many parents?
questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction involves only one parent — offspring are genetically identical clones.

questions.question: Main advantage of sexual reproduction over asexual
questions.explanation: Sexual reproduction mixes genes from two parents, creating variation that aids adaptation and evolution.

questions.question: Strawberry plants produce runners. This is
questions.explanation: Runners are horizontal stems that allow asexual vegetative propagation in strawberries.

questions.question: Gametes are produced by
questions.explanation: Meiosis is the special cell division that produces haploid gametes (sperm and eggs).

questions.question: Fertilisation is
options: fusion of male and female gametes
questions.answer: fusion of male and female gametes
questions.explanation: Fertilisation: sperm fuses with egg → zygote (diploid), which develops into a new organism.

questions.question: Which is NOT asexual reproduction?
questions.explanation: Fertilisation involves fusion of two gametes from different parents — it is sexual reproduction.

questions.question: Yeast reproduces asexually by
questions.explanation: Yeast forms a small bud that grows and eventually separates as a new cell.

questions.question: Why can disease wipe out all members of an asexually reproducing population?
options: All are genetically identical — no variation
questions.answer: All are genetically identical — no variation
questions.explanation: Without genetic variation, if one individual is susceptible to a pathogen, all offspring are equally susceptible.

questions.question: A potato eye growing into a new plant is
questions.explanation: Potato tubers have 'eyes' (buds) that can sprout and grow into new potato plants — asexual propagation.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: The human reproductive system produces gametes and supports development of offspring. Males produce sperm; females produce eggs and support the fetus during pregnancy.

Male reproductive system:
• Testes: produce sperm (spermatogenesis) and testosterone
• Epididymis: sperm mature and are stored
• Vas deferens: carries sperm to urethra
• Seminal vesicles, prostate: produce semen fluid
• Penis: deposits semen in vagina

Female reproductive system:
• Ovaries: produce eggs (oogenesis) and oestrogen/progesterone
• Fallopian tubes (oviducts): carry egg to uterus; fertilisation occurs here
• Uterus: where fetus develops
• Cervix, vagina: birth canal

content.title: Menstrual Cycle
content.body:
~28 day cycle controlled by hormones:
Day 1-5: menstruation (uterine lining shed)
Day 6-13: follicle develops, oestrogen rises, lining rebuilds
Day 14: ovulation (egg released from ovary)
Day 15-28: progesterone rises, lining thickens for implantation
If no fertilisation: progesterone falls → menstruation begins again

content.title: Pregnancy
content.body:
Fertilisation in fallopian tube → zygote → embryo travels to uterus
Implantation in uterine wall (~day 7)
Placenta forms: exchanges nutrients/waste between mother and fetus
Gestation: 9 months (40 weeks)
Birth: oxytocin stimulates contractions

questions.question: Sperm are produced in the
questions.explanation: Testes (in the scrotum) produce sperm cells by spermatogenesis and also produce testosterone.

questions.question: Fertilisation in humans normally occurs in the
questions.explanation: The egg is released from the ovary and fertilisation by sperm typically occurs in the fallopian tube (oviduct).

questions.question: Ovulation occurs approximately on day
questions.explanation: In a 28-day cycle, ovulation (egg release) occurs around day 14, triggered by LH surge.

questions.question: The hormone that triggers ovulation is
questions.explanation: Luteinising hormone (LH) surge from the pituitary gland triggers ovulation on approximately day 14.

questions.question: The placenta function is
options: exchange nutrients and waste between mother and fetus
questions.answer: exchange nutrients and waste between mother and fetus
questions.explanation: Placenta: oxygen and nutrients pass from mother's blood to fetus; CO2 and waste go the other way.

questions.question: Menstruation occurs because
options: progesterone falls causing uterine lining to shed
questions.answer: progesterone falls causing uterine lining to shed
questions.explanation: If no fertilisation, corpus luteum degenerates, progesterone drops, and the uterine lining is shed.

questions.question: Progesterone during the menstrual cycle mainly
options: maintains the uterine lining for implantation
questions.answer: maintains the uterine lining for implantation
questions.explanation: Progesterone in the second half of the cycle maintains the thick uterine lining ready for a fertilised egg.

questions.question: Human gestation period is approximately
questions.explanation: Human pregnancy lasts approximately 40 weeks (~9 months) from fertilisation to birth.

questions.question: Sperm are stored and mature in the
questions.explanation: After production in the testes, sperm mature and are stored in the epididymis before ejaculation.

questions.question: Oestrogen in the menstrual cycle mainly
options: rebuild the uterine lining and trigger LH surge
questions.answer: rebuild the uterine lining and trigger LH surge
questions.explanation: Oestrogen rises in the first half of the cycle, rebuilding the uterine lining and causing the LH surge that triggers ovulation.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: Flowering plants reproduce sexually using flowers. Pollination transfers pollen (male gametes) to the stigma. Fertilisation occurs in the ovule, producing seeds.

Parts of a flower:
• Stamen: anther (pollen) + filament — MALE
• Carpel: stigma + style + ovary (with ovules) — FEMALE
• Petals: attract insects
• Sepals: protect bud

Pollination types:
• Self-pollination: pollen to own flower
• Cross-pollination: pollen to different plant (promotes variation)

content.title: Insect vs Wind Pollination
content.body:
Insect-pollinated flowers:
• Brightly coloured petals
• Sweet scent and nectar
• Sticky/rough pollen (clings to insects)
• Sticky stigma inside flower

Wind-pollinated flowers:
• Small, dull petals (or none)
• No scent/nectar
• Large amounts of light, smooth pollen
• Feathery/long stigmas outside flower (catch pollen)

content.title: Seed Dispersal
content.body:
Seeds must spread to avoid competition with parent plant:
• Wind: wings (sycamore), parachutes (dandelion)
• Animals (eating): berries, fruits with hooks
• Water: coconut (floats)
• Explosive: pea pods split
• Animals (external): burrs, hooks on fur

questions.question: The male part of a flower is the
questions.explanation: The stamen (anther + filament) is the male reproductive organ, producing pollen.

questions.question: Pollination is
options: transfer of pollen from anther to stigma
questions.answer: transfer of pollen from anther to stigma
questions.explanation: Pollination is the transfer of pollen (containing male gametes) to the stigma of a flower.

questions.question: Cross-pollination is better than self-pollination because
questions.explanation: Cross-pollination combines genes from two different plants, creating offspring with more genetic variation.

questions.question: Which feature is characteristic of wind-pollinated flowers?
questions.explanation: Wind-pollinated plants have large feathery stigmas that hang outside to catch airborne pollen.

questions.question: Insect-pollinated flowers have sticky pollen because
options: it sticks to insects for transport
questions.answer: it sticks to insects for transport
questions.explanation: Sticky, rough pollen grains cling to the bodies of visiting insects and are carried to the next flower.

questions.question: After fertilisation the ovule becomes
questions.explanation: After fertilisation, the ovule develops into a seed containing the embryo and food store.

questions.question: The ovary wall develops into
questions.explanation: After fertilisation, the ovary wall surrounding the ovule develops into the fruit.

questions.question: Dandelion seeds are dispersed by
questions.explanation: Dandelion seeds have parachute-like structures (pappus) that catch wind and carry seeds away.

questions.question: Seed dispersal is important to prevent
options: overcrowding and competition with parent plant
questions.answer: overcrowding and competition with parent plant
questions.explanation: Dispersal spreads seeds away from the parent, reducing competition for light, water, and minerals.

questions.question: The stigma's role in reproduction is
questions.explanation: The stigma is the sticky top of the carpel that receives pollen during pollination.

questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

content.body: Living organisms grow and develop throughout their lives. Growth involves an increase in size and complexity. Development involves differentiation of cells into specialised types.

Growth measurements:
• Height, mass, length (simple)
• Dry mass: most accurate (removes water variation)
• Cell number

S-shaped (sigmoid) growth curve:
1. Lag phase: slow growth, organisms adapting
2. Log (exponential) phase: rapid growth
3. Stationary phase: birth rate = death rate
4. Decline phase: death rate > birth rate

content.title: Human Growth Stages
content.body:
Infancy (0-2): rapid growth, brain development
Childhood (2-12): steady growth
Puberty (11-16 approx): growth spurt, sexual maturation
Adulthood (18-60): stable size
Old age (60+): tissue deterioration

Growth hormone (GH) from pituitary controls growth rate

content.title: Metamorphosis
content.body:
Complete metamorphosis (holometabolism):
Egg → Larva → Pupa → Adult
Examples: butterfly, beetle, fly

Incomplete metamorphosis:
Egg → Nymph → Adult (nymph resembles adult)
Examples: grasshopper, cockroach

questions.question: Dry mass is the most accurate growth measure because
options: it removes the variable of water content
questions.answer: it removes the variable of water content
questions.explanation: Water content varies (dehydration etc.), so dry mass (after drying) gives a more consistent measure of actual biological material.

questions.question: In the log phase of a growth curve
questions.explanation: Log (exponential) phase: unlimited resources allow maximum growth rate — population doubles repeatedly.

questions.question: The stationary phase in a growth curve occurs when
questions.explanation: At stationary phase, population size stays constant because birth rate = death rate (limiting factors balance).

questions.question: Growth hormone is produced by the
questions.explanation: Growth hormone (GH/somatotropin) is produced and secreted by the anterior pituitary gland.

questions.question: Complete metamorphosis sequence is
questions.explanation: Complete (holometabolous) metamorphosis: Egg → Larva (e.g. caterpillar) → Pupa (chrysalis) → Adult

questions.question: A grasshopper undergoes incomplete metamorphosis. Its young are called
questions.explanation: In incomplete metamorphosis, juveniles are called nymphs and resemble miniature adults without wings.

questions.question: Puberty is characterised by
questions.explanation: Puberty: rapid growth spurt, development of secondary sexual characteristics, and reproductive maturity.

questions.question: Cell differentiation means
options: cells become specialised for particular functions
questions.answer: cells become specialised for particular functions
questions.explanation: Differentiation: cells develop specific structures and functions (e.g. muscle cells, nerve cells) from unspecialised stem cells.

questions.question: The lag phase at start of a growth curve represents
options: organisms adapting and preparing for growth
questions.answer: organisms adapting and preparing for growth
questions.explanation: During lag phase, organisms are synthesising enzymes and adjusting to their environment before rapid growth begins.

questions.question: Which hormone controls the growth of the body?
questions.explanation: Growth hormone (GH) from the anterior pituitary stimulates protein synthesis and bone growth throughout childhood.

questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

content.body:
Flower parts:
Sepal: protects bud
Petal: attracts pollinators (colour/scent)
Stamen = anther + filament (makes pollen)
Carpel = stigma + style + ovary (receives pollen, contains ovule)

Pollination: transfer of pollen to stigma
Self-pollination: same flower/plant
Cross-pollination: different plant (better genetic variation)

Agents: insects, wind, water, animals

content.title: Fertilisation in Plants
content.body:
Double fertilisation (flowering plants):
1. Pollen tube grows down style to ovule
2. One sperm + egg → zygote (seed)
3. Second sperm + polar nuclei → endosperm (food)

Seed structure: embryo (plumule+radicle+cotyledons) + testa
Fruit: mature ovary wall
Dispersed by: wind (winged), animals (hooks/flesh), water, explosion

content.title: Insect vs Wind Pollinated
content.body:
Insect: large sticky pollen, large colourful petals, nectar, scented
Wind: small smooth light pollen, dull small petals, feathery stigmas, hang outside flower (grass, wheat)

questions.question: The anther produces
questions.explanation: Anther: male part of stamen. Contains pollen sacs where pollen grains (containing male gametes) develop

questions.question: Pollination is
options: transfer of pollen from anther to stigma
questions.answer: transfer of pollen from anther to stigma
questions.explanation: Pollination precedes fertilisation. Pollen must reach the stigma (landing pad)

questions.question: Cross-pollination is preferred over self-pollination because
options: it produces genetic variation in offspring
questions.answer: it produces genetic variation in offspring
questions.explanation: Cross-pollination: different genetic material combines → variation → better adaptation potential

questions.question: Wind-pollinated flowers have
options: feathery/large stigmas to catch airborne pollen
questions.answer: feathery/large stigmas to catch airborne pollen
questions.explanation: Wind-pollinated: inconspicuous flowers, light smooth pollen, large feathery stigmas hanging exposed

questions.question: Double fertilisation in flowering plants produces
questions.explanation: Unique to angiosperms: one sperm→embryo, second sperm→endosperm (triploid food tissue)

questions.question: Fruit is botanically defined as
questions.explanation: Botanical fruit = mature ovary. Includes tomatoes, cucumbers, beans (not just sweet fruits)

questions.question: Hooks and barbs on seeds/fruits aid dispersal by
questions.explanation: Burdock, grass seeds with hooks cling to animal fur — dispersed far from parent plant

questions.question: Cotyledons in a seed serve as
questions.explanation: Cotyledons (seed leaves): store starch/protein to fuel early germination before photosynthesis begins

questions.question: Germination requires
questions.explanation: Germination conditions: water (activates enzymes), O₂ (aerobic respiration for energy), warmth (enzyme activity)

questions.question: Radicle and plumule in embryo become
questions.explanation: Radicle→primary root (grows downward). Plumule→first shoot with leaves (grows upward)

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Reproduction. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Reproduction concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Reproduction builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Reproduction. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Reproduction. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Reproduction include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Reproduction. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).
